title: The Primitive Water-Source

features.name: the original well-mouth
features.desc-on-touch: a circular opening carved by hand generations before the motel stood; the water below reflects stars even in daylight, remembering times when no archive existed

features.name: the stone-marked path
features.desc-on-touch: a worn groove in the bedrock leading from one place to another; the stones have recorded every footstep of every person who came for water

features.name: the mineral deposit sanctuary
features.desc-on-touch: layers of stone built by centuries of water; each layer is a different year, a different mineral composition, a different drought or plenty

features.name: the temperature-memory stone
features.desc-on-touch: a standing rock warmed by deep earth; it remembers the water's constant coolness, and in remembering, keeps the water true

features.name: the root-reach downward
features.desc-on-touch: tree roots have broken through into this chamber, drinking alongside human travelers; root and stone and water negotiate endlessly

inscription: the first well needs no archive; it archives itself in every drawn bucket

file.name: Well_Maintenance_Records_Oldest_Section.txt
file.note: the earliest entries are not in any written language—just tally marks on stone; below them, someone has written: 'counting how many times this water was trusted'

reverie.title: REVERIE — the morning water-drawing, before the motel
narr: you both kneel at the stone lip
Leif draws the bucket up hand over hand,
his knuckles wet, glistening in first light.
'it's the same temperature as it was two hundred years ago,' he says.
'how do you know?' 'Because the stone remembers.'
the water tastes of nothing but perfect thirst.

exits.label: back up through the bedrock plumbing, where newer pipes still learn from the old
toward the spring-source, where water still rises
exits.to: NEW: the-upwelling-memory, where the water remembers being stone

exits.label: along the underground stream, following where the water goes next
exits.to: NEW: the-water-s-returning-path, where every river follows its own old channels
